headline: A Gallery Built on Market, Not Monuments
meta_description: MAG The Women Gallery launches with a radical approach: selling women's art instead of just advocating for it.
summary_short: A new virtual gallery is changing how women artists gain recognition—by connecting them directly to collectors willing to buy.
body:
Jean-Baptiste Bettencourt spent 37 years as a senior executive at L'Oréal before launching MAG The Women Gallery in 2024. His motivation was personal. Both his parents were artists, and he watched his mother gradually set aside her own work to support his father's career—no argument, just a slow fade where one person's creative life was treated as less important.

Women artists have long received fewer gallery shows, lower auction prices, and fewer retrospectives than their male peers. That gap is well documented. What's rare is someone who decides to actually build something different.

MAG is a virtual gallery showing paintings, sculptures, photographs, and works on paper by women artists globally. But what makes it distinctive isn't the mission statement—it's the method. Rather than starting with advocacy, Bettencourt started with the market.

Take Isabelle Debray. After years away from her practice due to limited recognition, she returned to her studio with the gallery's support. Her intimate, poetic works now reach collectors who might never have discovered them otherwise. That arc—stepping back, then stepping forward again—is exactly what MAG exists to interrupt.

The gallery represents artists like Vietnamese painter Tô Bich Hai, whose layered work examines identity and cultural heritage, and photographer Elizabeth Lennard, whose work has been shown at the Louvre and Grand Palais.

Visibility matters. But visibility without market access—without someone actually buying the work—doesn't pay rent or buy supplies. Bettencourt's bet is that real economic opportunity is what sustains artistic practice over time.
